items.q: Why do designers often use 5th to 95th percentile data instead of just the 50th?
items.correct: To accommodate the majority of users rather than just the average.
items.misconception: Because the 50th percentile is too unreliable.
items.why: Students think designing for the average user is best, but it excludes most people. The 5th-95th range covers 90% of users - designing only for the mean fails everyone above or below it.

items.q: Why can thermoplastics be recycled but thermosets cannot?
items.correct: Thermoplastics soften when heated and can be remoulded; thermosets have cross-links that lock the shape permanently.
items.misconception: Thermosets are too dangerous to recycle.
items.random_wrong: Thermoplastics are cheaper, so it is worth recycling them.
items.why: Students miss the chemistry. Thermosets undergo a one-way curing reaction creating cross-links; reheating them just degrades them without remelting. Thermoplastics have no cross-links so they remelt freely.

items.q: What does an LDR's resistance do as light intensity INCREASES?
items.why: Students assume 'more input = more resistance', but LDR resistance is INVERSELY proportional to light. Dark = high resistance; bright = low resistance. This is why LDRs detect darkness in circuits.

items.q: Whose 2005 Double Diamond models the design process?
items.why: Correct answer: UK Design Council. “IDEO” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which technique systematically alters product attributes?
items.why: Correct answer: SCAMPER. “TRIZ” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many diamonds are in the Double Diamond model?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 diamonds. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
